children.value: If you analyze data, a lot of your work will involve numbers / matrices

stands for “numerical Python” -- a library optimized for scientific computing: working with arrays and data collection, vector and matrix computations, mathematical functions, random number generators, etc.
Many other libraries have adopted and extended the API of numpy, e.g.:

my_list = list(range(1000000))
%timeit [x * 2 for x in my_list]

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: 175 ms ± 16.4 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)

children.value:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)  # seed for reproducibility
my_arr = np.arange(1000000)
%timeit my_arr * 2

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: 4.19 ms ± 455 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

children.label: Why are numpy ndarray (n-dimensional array) faster than lists

children.value: Remember how python lists can contain any type:
: Determining the size, shape, memory consumption, and data types of arrays
: Getting and setting the value of individual array elements
: Getting and setting smaller subarrays within a larger array
: Getting and setting multiple arbitrary elements at once
: Changing the shape of a given array
Numpy arrays allow one type only - constructors up-cast types
Specifying data types at array creation time

children.label: Specifying data types at array creation time

children.value:
two_d_data = [list(range(i, i + 3)) for i in [2, 4, 6]]
two_d_data

# Create a 3x5 floating-point array filled with ones
np.ones((2, 3), dtype=float)

# something like builtin range function
np.arange(0, 20, 2)

# Create an array of five values evenly spaced between 0 and 1
np.linspace(0, 1, 5)

# Create a 3x3 array of values uniformly distributed between 0 and 1
np.random.random((3, 3))

# Normally distributed random values, mean 0 and standard deviation 1
np.random.normal(0, 1, (2, 3))

# Create a 3x3 array of random integers in the interval [0, 10)
np.random.randint(0, 10, (3, 3))
Boolean (True or False) stored as a byte
Half precision float: sign bit, 5 bits exponent, 10 bits mantissa
Single precision float: sign bit, 8 bits exponent, 23 bits mantissa
Double precision float: sign bit, 11 bits exponent, 52 bits mantissa
not the most relevant data type for data scientists
Jake VanderPlas’ Python Data Science Handbook chapter
these types of data are best stored and dealt with in a

x1 = np.random.randint(10, size=6)  # One-dimensional array
x2 = np.random.randint(10, size=(3, 4))  # Two-dimensional array
x3 = np.random.randint(10, size=(3, 4, 5))  # Three-dimensional array
: the total size of the array

print("x3 ndim: ", x3.ndim)
print("x3 shape:", x3.shape)
print("x3 size: ", x3.size)
print("x3 dtype: ", x3.dtype)
print("x3 itemsize: ", x3.itemsize)
print("x3 nbytes: ", x3.nbytes)

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
x3 ndim:  3
x3 shape: (3, 4, 5)
x3 size:  60
x3 dtype:  int32
x3 itemsize:  4
x3 nbytes:  240

children.value: Indexing is just the same as python list indexing:
You can extract subarrays using standard python List slicing:
Items can be accessed using a comma-separated tuple of indices:
Same as with lists and one-dimensional arrays:
x2[:3, ::2]  # all rows, every other column
This is different for subarrays: Those slices are

a = np.array([1,2,3])
aa = a[:2]
aa[0] = 99
print("a: ", a, "\naa: ", aa)
You can efficiently index arrays using boolean masks

text/plain.content: array([False, False, False, False, False,  True,  True,  True,  True])

children.value: Can give you arbitrary subsets of arrays given a set of indices
Often you need to change the shape of an array; this is done with

x = np.array([1, 2, 3])
y = np.array([3, 2, 1])
np.concatenate([x, y])
Repeated Executions Are Slow In Python

children.label: Repeated Executions Are Slow In Python

children.value:
def compute_reciprocals(values):
    output = np.empty(len(values))
    for i in range(len(values)):
        output[i] = 1.0 / values[i]
    return output
        
values = np.random.randint(1, 10, size=5)
compute_reciprocals(values)

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: 3.99 s ± 433 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

children.value: Python’s defacult implementation CPython is slow for repeated executions
This is mostly due to the fact that it’s not compiled down to bytecode
Major Bottlenecks are: type-checking and function dispatches
Various attempts to address this weakness:
, converts Python code to compilable C code
, converts snippets of Python code to fast LLVM bytecode.
None of these have reached broad adoption
is fast since it allows to
For many tasks you can use plain python and
1.0 / values  # vectorized, fast reciprocal computation with ufuncs

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: 5.07 ms ± 522 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

children.value: Most arithmetic operations are available as ufuncs

children.label: Most arithmetic operations are available as ufuncs

children.value:
x = np.arange(4)
print("x     =", x)
print("x + 5 =", x + 5)
print("x - 5 =", x - 5)
print("x * 2 =", x * 2)
print("x / 2 =", x / 2)
print("x // 2 =", x // 2)  # floor division
When working with large data sets, aggregations help to understand your data:
Again, python itself is slow at that, but

big_array = np.random.rand(1000000)
big_list = big_array.tolist()
%timeit sum(big_list)
%timeit np.sum(big_array)

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
9.83 ms ± 1.56 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
671 μs ± 80.8 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each)

children.value: Evaluate whether any elements are true
Evaluate whether all elements are true
You can find all the linear algebra functionalitites from the

array_to_save = np.arange(10)
np.save('some_array.npy', array_to_save)  # .npy file extension will be added if not specified

saved_array = np.load('some_array.npy')  # must specify .npy file extention
saved_array
